message.content: Wait, do I want padding-inline-start or scroll-padding-inline?

content.thinking:
The user is asking about the difference between `padding-inline-start` and `scroll-padding-inline` for their use case.

They want the first item to appear offset from the left edge, aligned with a grid column. There are two ways to think about this:

1. `padding-inline-start` on the `<ol>` — this adds actual space inside the scroll container before the first item. The scroll content starts further in.

2. `scroll-padding-inline` — this defines the snap alignment inset. It affects where items snap to, but doesn't add visual space.

They probably want both, actually. 

Using `padding-inline-start` creates the visual offset so the first item lands at the right grid position, while `scroll-padding-inline-start` should match that same value so snapping respects the padded edge rather than the container's edge.

content.text: Both, actually. `padding-inline-start` creates the visual space before the first item. `scroll-padding-inline-start` tells the snap algorithm to snap to that padded edge. Set them to the same value so the snap points line up with the visible content area.

message.content: hrm, this is tough. My g-main grid template columns are flexible, so this start padding needs to be too to match it, but not sure how to get that

content.thinking:
The user's grid uses `grid-template-columns: repeat(var(--num-cols, 1), 1fr)` — so the columns are `1fr` units, which are flexible and depend on the container width. The `--g-max-*` variables use the fixed `--g-col` value (140/16 rem), but the actual rendered column width will differ because `1fr` distributes available space.

So you can't just use `--g-max-1 + --g-gutter + --g-padding-h` because `--g-max-1` is a fixed value, not the actual flexible column width.
To find the actual column width at any viewport, I need to calculate it dynamically based on the container width, number of columns, gutters, and padding. The formula accounts for how `1fr` distributes the remaining space after accounting for gaps and padding, then I can use that to determine where any given column starts.

Since the grid container is full-width, I can use `100vw` as the container width in a CSS calculation, then derive the column width and use it to compute offsets to specific columns.

Writing CSS variables... on the `.g-main` containers themselves, not globally. So I need to figure out where that variable gets defined and whether I can reference it from outside the grid context. The real issue is that the versions scroller needs to align with column 2 of a grid system where the number of columns changes at different breakpoints. Let me think about the math: if the grid has N equal columns with gaps between them, column 2 starts at the width of one column plus the gutter, and I need to express that as a padding or offset value that works outside the grid container. Writing CSS formula...

The approach would be to compute the column offset dynamically using CSS custom properties, but that requires `--num-cols` to be defined at the root level or within specific breakpoints so it's available across the stylesheet. I should explain this situation to the user and walk through the practical solution.

content.text:
Right — the grid columns are `1fr`, so their actual width depends on the viewport. You can derive it though. For a full-width grid, 1 column width is:

```
(100vw - 2 * padding - (num-cols - 1) * gutter) / num-cols
```

So the offset to where column 2 starts would be:

```css
calc(var(--g-padding-h) + (100vw - 2 * var(--g-padding-h) - (var(--num-cols) - 1) * var(--g-gutter)) / var(--num-cols) + var(--g-gutter))
```

The catch is you'd need `--num-cols` available — right now it's only set on individual `.g-main` elements, not at `:root`. Want me to check what `--num-cols` is set to at the relevant breakpoints?

file.content:
--- layout: layout-main.webc title: About Tyler Gaw and this Site meta: image: /images/social-about.png photos: [ { key: "skateboarding", alt: "A Polaroid photo of my skateboard laying on a rug in my apartment", }, { key: "records", alt: "A Polaroid photo of my record collection in the 9 travel cases I store them", }, { key: "nyc", alt: "A Polaroid photo the lower Manhattan skyline looking Southeast on East Broadway in Chinatown", }, ] ---

Since 2006 I’ve made a living as a Designer and Software Engineer, creating world-class digital experiences for humans. In that time, I’ve been fortunate to work with excellent people at a few companies, as a freelancer, and as a founder.

I’m trying to use my skills and experience to put good into the world.

On Design

There are plenty of words and acronyms you can put in front of “Designer”. Product, Web, Graphic, UX, UI, IA, etc. The lines between each are blurry, and the titles go in and out of fashion. Depending on the project and team I’m working alongside, I practice them all to varying degrees. I prefer to call myself; “A Designer.”

On Engineering

Code is a means to an end. I have ideas and use code to bring them to life. That doesn’t mean I approach programming as a craft with any less enthusiasm or care as Design. I push myself to learn new languages, understand new patterns, and keep growing as an engineer. No language is off limits and I don’t avoid trying to learn difficult concepts.

Being a better designer makes me a better engineer and being a better engineer makes me a better designer. I get asked a lot which I want to do more; design or write code? I answer, “yes.”

About me

Designing and writing code get me money so I can have food so they get a lot of my attention. This is a non-exhaustive list of other things I love;

  • Skateboarding. I got my first board in 1997 for my 12th birthday. In 24 years the longest I've ever gone without it was 7 months. That was due to knee surgery after a torn ACL and meniscus. Your first assumption about how I did that will be correct. Skateboarding altered my trajectory in life. I would be a completely different person without it.

  • Vinyl records. I adore music of all types. Having them in a physical form is good for my soul. My collection grows at a comical pace. Spending an hour digging through bins in a record store is one of my favorite ways to clear my head. And then spend too much money.

  • New York City. This is my Home. I’m a part of it and it’s a part of me. I moved here in 2008. I’ve lived in 8 different apartments in Manhattan and Brooklyn. I’ve been here through multiple hurricanes, an earthquake, blizzards, breakups, tickets, an arrest, court dates, jury duty (twice), bar fights, terrorist attacks, and most recently, the deafening quiet, eerie stillness, reefer trucks full of bodies on 2nd AVE, and the brain altering trauma of a global pandemic. I love this city and it allows me to exist here. That’s the most anyone can ask of it.

About this site

I deployed the first Flash version of this site Summer of 2006. I built v1 during the last months of college to help land my first web design job. Fun tidbit, there’s a CD-ROM version of that first site. An interface built with Macromedia Director on CD-ROM was the required final project deliverable at that time.

Since then, I’ve made iterations on the site every few years to accomplish different goals. I’ve changed the design, I’ve changed the work, and most important of all I’ve changed. This site is a lot of things. A portfolio of design work. A blog. A place to experiement. Above all else, this site is a reflection of me.

For this current iteration I’m using;

  • Eleventy
  • Netlify
  • CSS: The kind that comes in the browser
  • JavaScript: The regular kind that comes in the browser
  • Set in Proto Grotesk

Version history

The source for all versions is available on GitHub.
